items.passage: Mycorrhizal networks are vast underground systems formed by fungi connecting plant roots across forests. These threadlike fungal connections may extend for many meters beneath the surface, linking trees of different species. Researchers have observed that nutrients and chemical signals travel through these networks between connected plants. Older trees often share sugars with younger seedlings struggling in the shade. However, the full importance of these hidden partnerships is still being investigated by ecologists worldwide.

Urban heat islands are city areas significantly warmer than the surrounding countryside. Dark surfaces like asphalt are heated throughout the day and slowly release stored heat at night. Tall buildings tend to trap warm air and block natural cooling breezes. Limited vegetation also reduces shade and the cooling effects produced by plant transpiration. However, planting trees and adding reflective rooftops may help cities significantly reduce these temperature differences during summer months.

Pulsars are formed from massive stars that have collapsed under their own enormous gravity. These compact rotating objects emit narrow beams of radio waves from their magnetic poles. When the rotating beams sweep across Earth, sensitive telescopes can detect the regular pulses of energy. Their extreme density makes them spin with remarkable precision, sometimes thousands of times per second. However, astronomers may discover that pulsars provide reliable cosmic clocks for testing fundamental physics theories.
